did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an improved Deformable DETR model with an Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) module integrated into a ResNet-50 backbone for skin lesion detection in melanoma. The study involves training this deep learning object detection model on a dataset of 650 melanoma images to achieve classification and localization results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing an improved Deformable DETR model with an Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) module for melanoma detection. It details a specific methodology, uses a dataset of 650 images, trains the model for 150 epochs, and reports new quantitative results (AP, AP50, AP75, Recall) comparing the proposed model against baselines like Faster R-CNN and RetinaNet. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCKE), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Enhanced Melanoma Detection: An Improved Deformable DETR Model with Efficient Channel Attention
journal: 2025 15th International Conference on Computer and Knowledge Engineering (ICCKE)
brief_summary: This study proposes an improved Deformable DETR model integrated with an Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) module into a ResNet-50 backbone to enhance skin lesion detection for melanoma. The model was trained and evaluated on a dataset of 650 melanoma images sourced from Roboflow, split into training, validation, and test sets. The experimental framework utilized PyTorch 2.0 and was trained for 150 epochs on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2060 GPU. Evaluation metrics included Average Precision (AP), AP50, AP75, and Recall, comparing the proposed model against baselines such as Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, and standard Deformable DETR. The proposed model achieved an AP of 53.4%, an AP50 of 61.3%, an AP75 of 55.1%, and a Recall of 75.0%, outperforming the baseline Deformable DETR which scored 52.6% AP and 73.7% Recall. These results indicate that integrating ECA significantly improves feature extraction and detection accuracy for melanoma lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on detecting skin lesions in melanoma cancer. The dataset from Roboflow includes classes labeled as 'Cancer', 'Acral Lentiginous', 'Lentigo Maligna', and 'Nodular Melanoma'. These are specific subtypes or general labels for melanoma used in the object detection task.

all_task_types.target: Bounding boxes and class labels for melanoma skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes an improved Deformable DETR model for 'skin lesion detection in melanoma'. It uses object detection metrics such as Average Precision (AP, AP50, AP75) and Recall. The model predicts bounding box coordinates (using L1 and GIoU losses) and class probabilities (using cross-entropy loss) for classes including 'Cancer', 'Acral Lentiginous', 'Lentigo Maligna', and 'Nodular Melanoma'.

datasets_used.name: Roboflow Melanoma Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset sourced from Roboflow specifically designed for object detection tasks related to melanoma skin cancer. It is available in multiple annotation formats including YOLO and COCO JSON.

use.training: Used to train the improved Deformable DETR model with ECA module for 150 epochs.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final model performance (AP, AP50, AP75, Recall) and compare against baselines like Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, and standard Deformable DETR.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using point estimates of metrics (AP, AP50, AP75, Recall) without statistical significance analysis.

Roboflow Melanoma Dataset.notes: The proposed model (Ours) achieved an AP of 53.4%, representing a 0.8% relative improvement over the baseline Deformable DETR (52.6%). It also showed higher AP50 (61.3% vs 58.9%) and AP75 (55.1% vs 53.1%) scores. The recall improved to 75.0%, surpassing the baseline's 73.7%. The model outperformed RetinaNet and standard DETR variants, and demonstrated competitive performance with Faster R-CNN.

architecture_used.main_model: Improved Deformable DETR with Efficient Channel Attention (ECA)

architecture_details.backbone_modification: Integrated Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) module into the ResNet backbone
architecture_details.encoder_decoder: Transformer encoder-decoder architecture with multi-scale deformable attention modules
architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Multi-scale deformable attention for sparse key sampling points around reference points

eca_module_details.operation: Global average pooling followed by 1D convolution with adaptive kernel size k = C/r (where r is typically 2) and softmax normalization
eca_module_details.purpose: Adaptively adjust channel weights to focus on important features and suppress less relevant ones

training_details.hardware: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2060 GPU (6 GB VRAM)

loss_function.total_loss: L_total = λ1 * L_cls + λ2 * L_box + λ3 * L_match
loss_function.bounding_box_loss: Combination of L1 loss and GIoU loss (L_box = α * L_L1 + β * (1 - GIoU))
loss_function.matching_loss: Bipartite matching loss using Hungarian algorithm (L_match)
loss_function.coefficients: λ1, λ2, λ3 for loss weighting; α, β for bounding box loss weighting (specific values not reported)

classification_or_output_head.output: Bounding box coordinates and class probabilities for classes: Cancer, Acral Lentiginous, Lentigo Maligna, Nodular Melanoma

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Improved Deformable DETR with Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) module
conclusions: The proposed improved Deformable DETR model with an integrated Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) module into the ResNet-50 backbone achieves superior performance in melanoma skin lesion detection compared to baseline models.
The model achieved an Average Precision (AP) of 53.4%, representing a 0.8% relative improvement over the baseline Deformable DETR (52.6%).

The proposed model demonstrated higher detection accuracy across varying IoU thresholds, with AP50 of 61.3% and AP75 of 55.1%, outperforming baselines such as Faster R-CNN, RetinaNet, and standard DETR.
The model achieved a Recall of 75.0%, surpassing the baseline Deformable DETR's 73.7%, indicating a reduction in false negatives.
Integrating the lightweight ECA module effectively enhances feature extraction by adaptively adjusting channel weights to focus on important features and suppress less relevant ones.
The study validates the effectiveness of incorporating attention mechanisms like ECA into transformer-based object detection frameworks for medical imaging tasks.
The improved model demonstrates competitive performance with established architectures like Faster R-CNN (ResNet-50/110) while offering advantages in feature discrimination and localization accuracy.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset of only 650 images (455 training, 130 validation, 65 test), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results.
The dataset was sourced from Roboflow rather than a standardized, widely recognized dermatological benchmark (e.g., ISIC), raising concerns about population bias and external validation issues.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal test split of the single source dataset.

The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the performance improvements, making it unclear if the observed gains (e.g., 0.8% AP improvement) are statistically significant.
The absolute performance metrics are relatively low (AP of 53.4%), suggesting that the model may still struggle with detection accuracy in clinical settings.
Specific hyperparameters for loss weighting coefficients (alpha, beta, lambda1, lambda2, lambda3) and the ECA reduction ratio (r) are not explicitly reported in the text.
The paper does not discuss potential data leakage risks or detailed preprocessing steps beyond the dataset split, which is critical for reproducibility in medical imaging.

Deployment concerns are not addressed, particularly regarding the computational cost and latency of the proposed model compared to lighter alternatives like YOLO or MobileNet, which are often preferred for clinical deployment.
